Hemoglobin.description: Hemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen throughout your body.
recommended_actions_low: Check iron levels (ferritin, iron panel)
Evaluate for sources of bleeding
Consider iron supplementation
Increase iron-rich foods (red meat, spinach, beans)

Serum Iron.description: Serum iron measures the amount of iron circulating in your blood. It reflects how much iron is immediately available to your body for making red blood cells.
recommended_actions_low: Eat iron-rich foods at every meal (lentils, spinach, lean red meat, fortified cereals)
Pair iron foods with Vitamin C to boost absorption (lemon juice, bell peppers, orange juice)
Avoid tea and coffee within 1 hour of iron-rich meals — tannins block absorption
Ask your doctor about iron supplementation
Check for underlying causes such as heavy periods or GI bleeding

Ferritin.description: Ferritin is the storage form of iron in your body. It reflects your iron reserves — think of it as your iron 'savings account.' Low ferritin is often the earliest sign of iron deficiency, even before anemia develops.
low_causes: Iron deficiency (most common cause in women)
Celiac disease or other malabsorption conditions
low_symptoms: Persistent fatigue even with adequate sleep
recommended_actions_low: Prioritize iron-rich foods daily: red lentils, chickpeas, tofu, fortified oatmeal, dark leafy greens
Cook in a cast iron pan — it adds small amounts of iron to food
Discuss oral iron supplementation with your doctor (ferrous sulfate is common)
Retest ferritin in 8-12 weeks after starting dietary changes or supplements
Discuss whether heavy menstrual periods may be the underlying cause

TIBC.description: TIBC (Total Iron Binding Capacity) measures how well your blood can transport iron. When iron stores are low, your body produces more transport proteins to grab any available iron — so TIBC goes up when iron is low.
high_causes: Iron deficiency anemia (most common cause of high TIBC)
recommended_actions_high: High TIBC combined with low serum iron and low ferritin confirms iron deficiency — focus on replenishing iron stores
Follow dietary and supplementation guidance for iron deficiency
Retest the full iron panel (iron, ferritin, TIBC) together after 8-12 weeks of treatment

Transferrin Saturation.description: Transferrin saturation shows what percentage of your iron transport proteins are actually carrying iron. A low percentage means your transport proteins are mostly empty — a clear sign that iron is in short supply.
recommended_actions_low: Combine results with ferritin and serum iron for complete picture
Address dietary iron intake and discuss supplements with your doctor
Investigate any ongoing blood loss

MCV.description: MCV (Mean Corpuscular Volume) measures the average size of your red blood cells. Small red blood cells (low MCV) are a classic sign of iron deficiency — your body makes smaller cells when it doesn't have enough iron to fill them.
recommended_actions_low: Low MCV alongside low ferritin and serum iron confirms iron deficiency anemia
Iron supplementation and dietary changes typically normalize MCV over 2-3 months
Rule out thalassemia trait if iron stores are normal despite low MCV

Glucose (Fasting).description: Fasting glucose measures blood sugar levels after not eating for 8-12 hours. It screens for diabetes and prediabetes.
recommended_actions_high: May need oral diabetes medication or insulin

Total Cholesterol.description: Total cholesterol is the sum of LDL (bad), HDL (good), and other lipids in your blood. High levels increase heart disease risk.
high_symptoms: Can lead to heart attack or stroke over time
recommended_actions_high: Reduce saturated fat and trans fats
Consider statin therapy if risk is elevated

LDL Cholesterol.description: LDL is 'bad' cholesterol that can build up in arteries and increase heart disease risk.
high_causes: Diet high in saturated and trans fats
recommended_actions_high: Reduce saturated fats to <7% of calories
Consider statin if cardiovascular risk is high

Triglycerides.description: Triglycerides are a type of fat in blood. High levels increase risk of heart disease and pancreatitis.
high_causes: Excess calories, especially from sugar and alcohol
recommended_actions_high: May need fibrate or niacin medication if very high

Vitamin D.description: Vitamin D helps absorb calcium and supports bone health, immune function, and mood.
recommended_actions_low: Vitamin D supplementation (1000-2000 IU daily typical)
Increase sun exposure (15-30 min daily)
Eat vitamin D rich foods (fatty fish, fortified milk)
Target level: 30-50 ng/mL

TSH.description: TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone) regulates thyroid function. Abnormal levels indicate thyroid problems.
recommended_actions_high: Get full thyroid panel (Free T4, Free T3, antibodies)
Recheck TSH in 6-8 weeks after starting treatment
